description: Rules for gear itself: weapon and armor properties, magic and signature items, crafting, wear and depletion, and how equipment enters and leaves play.

gear.description: Rules defining the properties, categories, and special natures of items.

focuses.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons carry property tags such as reach, two-handed, loading, or messy that grant situational rules instead of bare damage numbers (as in D&D 5e properties or PbtA weapon tags).
focuses.armor_types: Armor is categorized into types with distinct protection, cost, and penalties to stealth, speed, or skills, making protection a tradeoff.
focuses.quality_tiers: Items come in craftsmanship grades such as poor, standard, fine, and masterwork that modify performance and price independent of magic.
focuses.item_rarity: Items are classed by rarity from common to legendary, gating availability, pricing, and expected character power (as in D&D 5e magic item rarity).
focuses.magic_item_attunement: Characters must attune to magic items to use their powers and can maintain only a limited number of attunements, capping how items stack (as in D&D 5e).
focuses.cursed_items: Some items carry hidden curses revealed through use, punishing greed and making identification matter.
focuses.artifact_rules: Artifacts are singular items with campaign-scale powers, their own agendas or costs, and rules for resisting or escaping their influence.
focuses.sentient_items: Items with minds have personalities, goals, and means of communication, and may contest control with their wielder (as in D&D's sentient weapons).
focuses.shield_sacrifice: A shield can be sacrificed to negate a blow entirely, converting equipment into a one-use survival resource (the OSR 'shields shall be splintered' rule).
focuses.signature_items: A character's signature item grows alongside them, gaining traits or powers through play rather than being replaced (as in The One Ring's rewards for famous weapons).
focuses.special_materials: Weapons and armor made of special materials such as silver, cold iron, or mithral interact with resistances and weight rules, making composition tactical.
focuses.trinket_tables: Random tables issue small evocative possessions with no fixed mechanical use, seeding roleplay and improvised utility (as in D&D 5e's trinkets).
focuses.tool_kits: Task-specific kits such as lockpicks, healer's bags, and climbing gear are required to attempt certain tasks, with quality or charges mattering.
focuses.consumable_items: Single-use items like potions, scrolls, and bombs deliver strong effects that leave play when used, creating hoarding dilemmas.
focuses.item_identification: Unknown items must be identified through spells, specialists, or risky experimentation before their properties are known.

crafting.description: Rules for making, brewing, enchanting, and recycling items.

focuses.recipe_crafting: Items are crafted from known recipes specifying required materials, tools, and time, making crafting deterministic once inputs are secured.
focuses.recipe_discovery: New recipes, formulas, or blueprints are learned by finding, buying, or experimenting, expanding what can be made.
focuses.material_harvesting: Raw materials such as ores, herbs, and hides are gathered from the environment, with quality and quantity set by rolls and terrain.
focuses.monster_part_harvesting: Slain creatures can be butchered for components, with checks determining what survives extraction and links hunting to crafting (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.enchanting: Mundane items are imbued with magical properties through rituals, rare components, and costs, with rules for limits and failures.
focuses.alchemy_potions: Potions, elixirs, and volatile concoctions are brewed from ingredients, often with side effects or potency rolls.
focuses.poison_crafting: Poisons are brewed and applied under rules for potency, delivery method, onset time, and the legal or moral hazards of use.
focuses.smithing_tests: Forging is resolved through skill tests where margins of success determine quality, flaws, or masterwork results (as in Forbidden Lands' crafting talents).
focuses.salvage_scrapping: Items can be broken down into parts and materials, recycling loot into crafting inputs.
focuses.crafting_stations: Crafting requires access to appropriate facilities such as a forge, laboratory, or workshop, making infrastructure part of the crafting economy.
focuses.cooking_provisions: Meals and provisions are prepared from ingredients, granting buffs or morale and preserving food that would otherwise spoil.

durability.description: Rules for wear, breakage, and the depletion of ammunition and charges.

focuses.item_wear: Items accumulate wear through use and mishap, degrading notch by notch until maintained or ruined.
focuses.weapon_breakage: Weapons can break outright on fumbles, brutal parries, or overuse, abruptly removing them from play (as in Torchbearer or fumble-table traditions).
focuses.armor_ablation: Armor absorbs harm by losing its own protective value, so defense is spent as a depleting buffer (as in Forbidden Lands' armor damage).
focuses.usage_dice: A consumable's supply is a die rolled when used that steps down on low results and vanishes at the bottom, abstracting quantity into risk (as in The Black Hack).
focuses.ammo_depletion_abstract: Ammunition is not counted per shot; instead flags, post-fight checks, or fictional cues determine when you run dry.
focuses.ammo_tracking: Every arrow, bolt, and bullet is counted and crossed off, with recovery rules for retrieving spent ammunition after fights.
focuses.charge_depletion: Wands, batteries, and devices hold limited charges expended per activation, sometimes with a risky roll on the final charge (as in D&D wands).
focuses.environmental_gear_damage: Acid, rust, fire, and immersion damage equipment itself, so hazards and monsters threaten inventory as well as bodies (as with D&D's rust monster).
focuses.weapon_jamming: Firearms and complex devices can jam, misfire, or overheat on bad rolls, requiring actions to clear and rewarding maintenance.

economy_of_gear.description: Rules for how equipment is acquired, slotted, and abstracted into play.

focuses.loadout_abstraction: Characters choose an abstract loadout level rather than itemizing gear, with heavier loads slowing them but promising more options.
focuses.quick_equipment_flashback: Characters can declare mid-mission that they packed an item via a flashback or resource spend, trading bookkeeping for a cost at the moment of need (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.starting_packages: New characters pick pre-bundled equipment packages by class or background, skipping itemized shopping at creation.
focuses.upgrade_slots: Items have limited upgrade or modification slots into which enhancements are installed, bounding how far gear can be improved (as in Gloomhaven's enhancements or weapon mod systems).
focuses.equip_slots: Characters can equip a limited number of items by body location such as head, body, and hands, so wearing gear is a constrained choice distinct from carrying it (as in Gloomhaven).
focuses.loot_decks: Loot is drawn from decks during play, randomizing rewards and letting acquisition happen at the table rather than from a price list (as in Gloomhaven's loot deck).
focuses.campaign_persistent_gear: Items unlock, improve, or persist across campaign sessions, with stickers, envelopes, or logs recording the arsenal's history (as in legacy board games).
focuses.availability_rolls: Whether an item can be found for sale is a roll based on settlement size, rarity, or law level, so markets vary by place (as in Traveller's availability throws).
focuses.equipment_drafting: Starting or scenario equipment is drafted from a shared pool, so players shape loadouts by picking against one another (a common board game setup).
